events.event: The body of Queen Victoria is carried into Portsmouth harbour on the royal yacht Alberta.
The British Labour Exchange, the precursor to the Jobcentre, opens for the first time.
The birth of Jackie Charlton, the legendary England World Cup winner and football manager.
British forces capture New Amsterdam, later renamed New York in honour of the Duke of York.
The birth of James Joyce; the Irish author's 'Ulysses' had a monumental impact on British literature.
The first public test of Robert Watson-Watt's radar system takes place near Daventry.
The London Daily Advertiser publishes the first ever stock exchange quotations.
The birth of Johnny Bragg; though American, his work influenced the UK's early rock and roll scene.
British Prime Minister Harold Macmillan makes the 'Wind of Change' speech in South Africa.
The birth of Archibald MacLeish; the poet's work was widely read in academic circles across the UK.
Malcolm Campbell sets a new world land speed record of 174 mph at Pendine Sands, Wales.
The Sunday Times Magazine is published for the first time, revolutionising British supplements.
Spanish and French forces defeat the British at the capture of Minorca.
The Royal Greenwich Observatory begins broadcasting the Greenwich Time Signal (the 'pips').
Parking meters are first installed in the UK, appearing on the streets of Mayfair, London.
Death of Lancelot 'Capability' Brown, the architect of Britain's most famous landscaped estates.
The death of King George VI and the accession of Queen Elizabeth II to the throne.
The Munich Air Disaster occurs, resulting in the deaths of eight Manchester United players.
Edward of Caernarvon becomes the first English Prince of Wales.
The birth of Charles Dickens in Portsmouth, the prolific chronicler of Victorian London.
An IRA mortar attack on 10 Downing Street takes place during a Cabinet meeting.
Mary, Queen of Scots, is beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle on the orders of Elizabeth I.
The birth of Addie McPhail; the actress was a familiar face in UK cinemas during the Golden Age.
The first execution by lethal gas in the US is reported widely as a curiosity in the British press.
The Beatles perform on the Ed Sullivan Show, cementing their international fame.
The birth of Tom Hiddleston, the acclaimed British stage and screen actor.
An IRA bomb explodes in London's Docklands, ending a seventeen-month ceasefire.
The St Scholastica's Day riot breaks out between students and locals in Oxford.
Queen Victoria marries Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha at St James's Palace.
The birth of Harold Macmillan, British Prime Minister who led during the post-war boom.
Margaret Thatcher is elected as the first female leader of the Conservative Party.
Nelson Mandela is released from prison; the news is celebrated across the UK.
The birth of John Surtees, the only person to win World Championships on both two and four wheels.
Lady Jane Grey, the 'Nine Days' Queen', is executed at the Tower of London.
The birth of Charles Darwin in Shrewsbury, the naturalist who proposed the theory of evolution.
The first public broadcast of 'The News' by the BBC in the UK.
The Declaration of Right is read out, confirming William and Mary as joint monarchs.
The Massacre of Glencoe takes place in the Scottish Highlands.
The birth of Robbie Williams, the record-breaking British singer-songwriter.
The death of Captain James Cook during a skirmish in Hawaii.
The Oxford Union carries the motion 'that this House will in no circumstances fight for its King and Country'.
Torvill and Dean win Olympic Gold for their Bolero routine, watched by 24 million British viewers.
The birth of John Anderson, the British character actor known for his work in theatre and television.
Decimal Day: Britain officially switches to a decimal currency system.
The largest protest in UK history takes place in London against the impending Iraq War.
The first ever recorded cheque in Britain is drawn for the sum of £400.
The birth of Sir Francis Galton, the polymath who founded modern statistics and meteorology.
The birth of Edgar Bergen; his ventriloquism was a major hit with UK radio listeners.
The Second Battle of St Albans takes place during the Wars of the Roses.
Moliere dies after a performance; his plays remain central to British theatrical education.
The European Communities Act passes in the House of Commons.
George, Duke of Clarence, is executed at the Tower, reportedly drowned in a butt of malmsey wine.
The birth of Mary I, the first queen regnant of England, in Greenwich.
The birth of Sir Bobby Robson, the beloved England football manager and mentor.
The bread riots in Liverpool occur due to food shortages and high prices.
The birth of Lee Marvin; the American star was a favourite in UK action cinema.
The birth of Prince Andrew, Duke of York, at Buckingham Palace.
The coronation of Edward VI takes place at Westminster Abbey.
Anthony Eden resigns as Foreign Secretary in protest against Neville Chamberlain's appeasement policy.
The birth of Jari Litmanen; his career at Liverpool brought him to prominence for UK fans.
Richard Trevithick's steam locomotive makes its first run on the Pen-y-Darren ironworks railway.
The Communist Manifesto is published in London by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels.
The birth of Alan Rickman in Hammersmith, the iconic stage and film actor.
The Battle of Fishguard begins, the last invasion of mainland Britain by French troops.
The birth of Margaret Wiggin; the writer's work was central to British home and lifestyle magazines.
Scientists in Scotland announce the successful cloning of Dolly the sheep.
The death of the English Romantic poet John Keats in Rome.
Lake Victoria is declared the source of the Nile by British explorer John Speke.
The birth of Helena Bonham Carter, the distinguished British character actress.
The steam locomotive Flying Scotsman begins its official service.
The birth of Michel Legrand; the composer's cinematic scores were highly influential in the UK.
The engagement of Prince Charles and Lady Diana Spencer is officially announced.
Pope Pius V excommunicates Queen Elizabeth I of England.
The death of Sir Christopher Wren, the architect of St Paul's Cathedral.
The birth of George Harrison in Liverpool, the lead guitarist of The Beatles.
The Bank of England issues the first ever one pound note.
Robert Watson-Watt and Arnold Wilkins demonstrate the first practical radar system.
The birth of Ole Gunnar Solskjaer; his impact as a player and manager at Manchester United is well known.
The British Parliament votes to abandon further military operations in the American War of Independence.
The Labour Representation Committee, later the Labour Party, is founded in London.
The birth of Jonathan Ive, the British designer responsible for many iconic Apple products.
The Relief of Ladysmith takes place during the Second Boer War.
Francis Crick and James Watson announce they have discovered the chemical structure of DNA.
The birth of Jelena Jankovic; the tennis star's matches were a staple of Wimbledon coverage.
French and Native American forces attack Deerfield, Massachusetts; the news impacts British colonial policy.
Hattie McDaniel becomes the first African American to win an Oscar; the event is a milestone discussed in UK film history.
The British government passes the Commonwealth Immigrants Act 1968.
